---
title: "AI Episode 13 — Vision-Language-Action (VLA)"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Explain what a VLA model is and how it differs from VLN.
- Describe the observation → action loop and common action representations.
- Wire a VLA policy to a ROS 2 control interface, safely.
- Evaluate a policy honestly, including out-of-distribution behavior.

**Teaching:** 45 min · **Exercises:** 30 min · **Builds on:** [Ep 12 — VLN](ai-12-vln.qmd), [Robotics Ep 3](03-ros2-navigation.qmd)
:::

## VLA vs VLN

In [VLN](ai-12-vln.qmd) the model emits high-level **goals** that a planner (Nav2) executes. A
**Vision-Language-Action** model goes further: it maps an observation and an instruction
**directly to low-level actions** — end-effector deltas, joint targets, or base velocities — and
runs in a tight closed loop.

| | VLN | VLA |
|---|-----|-----|
| Output | goal pose(s) | actions (continuous/tokenized) |
| Executor | classical planner + controller | the policy *is* the controller |
| Loop rate | per-goal (seconds) | per-step (10–50+ Hz) |
| Strength | reliable, interpretable | dexterous, reactive, end-to-end |
| Risk | brittle grounding | opaque, harder to bound |

Use VLN when a map + planner suffice; reach for VLA for contact-rich or reactive tasks
(manipulation, whole-body control) where hand-designed planning is hard.

## The observation → action loop

```
            ┌─────────────── policy (VLA model) ───────────────┐
image ─▶ vision encoder ─┐                                      │
                          ├─▶ fused tokens ─▶ action head ─▶ action(s)
instruction ─▶ text enc ─┘                                      │
            └───────────── conditioned on proprioception ───────┘
                         observe ◀── execute on robot ◀── action
```

```python
# Conceptual control loop (provider/model-agnostic)
policy = load_vla_policy(checkpoint)            # your trained/finetuned VLA
rate = Rate(20)                                 # 20 Hz control
while not done:
    obs = get_observation()                     # image(s), proprioception
    action = policy.act(obs, instruction)       # -> e.g. 7-DoF delta + gripper
    action = clamp_to_limits(action)            # SAFETY: never trust raw output
    send_action(action)                         # to the controller (see below)
    rate.sleep()
```

## Action representations

- **Continuous deltas** — e.g. end-effector `(dx, dy, dz, droll, dpitch, dyaw, gripper)`. Simple,
  common for manipulation.
- **Tokenized actions** — discretize each dimension into bins so a transformer predicts action
  "tokens" like text. Enables reusing LM architectures.
- **Action chunking** — predict a short *sequence* of future actions per inference, then execute
  them open-loop briefly. Reduces latency sensitivity and jitter.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
Have learners reason about **control rate vs inference latency**. If the model takes 80 ms but the
controller wants 20 Hz (50 ms budget), the naive loop stalls. Action chunking is the standard fix —
make them connect the dots rather than just stating it.
:::

## Wiring to ROS 2 — safely

A VLA outputs actions; ROS 2 carries them to the hardware. For a mobile base it's the same
`/cmd_vel` `Twist` from [Episode 3](03-ros2-navigation.qmd); for an arm it's a joint/trajectory
controller:

```python
def send_action(action):
    # Mobile base: reuse the Twist interface from Episode 3
    twist = Twist(linear=Vec3(x=action.v), angular=Vec3(z=action.w))
    cmd_vel_pub.publish(twist)
    # Arm: publish to a joint controller / FollowJointTrajectory action instead
```

**Safety is not optional** for a policy that drives hardware directly:

- **Clamp** every action to joint/velocity/torque limits before sending.
- **Watchdog**: if no fresh action arrives within N ms, command zero/hold.
- **E-stop & workspace bounds**: a hardware stop and software geofence the policy cannot override.
- **Confidence / OOD check**: detect inputs unlike training data and fall back to a safe stop.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge — Budget the loop
Your VLA inference takes 70 ms but the arm controller expects commands at 30 Hz (~33 ms period).
The naive loop can't keep up. Name one technique to bridge the gap and one risk it introduces.

### Solution
**Action chunking**: predict, say, 8 actions per inference and execute them at 30 Hz while the
next chunk computes — amortizing the 70 ms over many control steps. **Risk:** the chunk is
executed partly open-loop, so it can't react to disturbances mid-chunk; keep chunks short and
re-plan on significant observation change.
:::

## Evaluate honestly

As with [VLN](ai-12-vln.qmd), demos are not evidence. For VLA report:

- **Task success rate** over many trials and **seeds/initial conditions** — not a cherry-picked run.
- **Robustness**: success under perturbations (lighting, object pose, distractors).
- **Out-of-distribution behavior**: what does it do on inputs unlike training? Safe failure or
  dangerous flailing?
- A **failure taxonomy**: perception vs policy vs control vs safety-trip.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge — Pick the right tool
For each task, would you start with VLN or VLA, and why?
(a) "Drive to the loading dock." (b) "Pick up the red mug and hand it to me."

### Solution
(a) **VLN** — a map + planner reliably reaches a known location; goals are interpretable and
bounded. (b) **VLA** — contact-rich grasping and handover are reactive and hard to plan
classically; an end-to-end policy handles the closed-loop dexterity. The two compose: VLN to get
there, VLA to do the manipulation.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- VLA maps observation + instruction **directly to actions**; it *is* the controller.
- Mind action representation (continuous/tokenized) and **action chunking** for latency.
- Send actions through ROS 2, but **clamp, watchdog, geofence, and E-stop** — always.
- Evaluate over many trials with robustness + OOD tests and a failure taxonomy.
- VLN and VLA **compose**: navigate with VLN, manipulate with VLA.
:::

Next in this track: World Models for Planning (Ep 14) and Evaluation & Safety (Ep 15) — see the
[track map](https://github.com/FrankAsanteVanLaarhoven/ForgeLabCarpentries/blob/main/curriculum/tracks/ai-world-models.md).
